# THERMODYNAMIC PROPERTIES OF SELECTED SOLUTES

# IN LIQUID METAL SOLUTIONS*

David F. Bowersox

Los Alamos Scientific Laboratory, University of California Los Alamos, New Mexico 87544, United States of America

The partial molar enthalpies of solution, $\Delta \overline{\mathbf{H}_{\mathbf{i}}^{*}}$ , have been calculated for selected elements in liquid plutonium from solubility data over the temperature range 700 to $1000^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . The enthalpies of solution of vanadium, chromium, niobium, molybdenum, tantalum, and tungsten were calculated from regular solution theory by the equation

$$
\Delta \overline {{\mathrm {H}}} _ {\mathrm {i}} ^ {*} = \mathrm {T} \Delta \mathrm {S} _ {\mathrm {f}} - \mathrm {R T} \ln \mathrm {N} _ {\mathrm {i}},
$$

where $\mathbf{T}$ is the absolute temperature, $\Delta S_{\mathrm{f}}$ is the entropy of fusion of i at the normal melting temperature, $\mathbf{R}$ is the gas constant, and $\mathbf{N}_{\mathbf{i}}$ is the mole fraction of solute i in a saturated solution at temperature $\mathbf{T}$ . The enthalpies of solution of tungsten and tantalum in sixteen liquid rare-earth metals were also calculated by this equation. Solubility parameters, $\delta_{\mathrm{j}}$ , were calculated from these enthalpies by the formula

$$
\delta_ {\mathrm {i}} = \delta_ {\mathrm {B}} - \left(\frac {\Delta \bar {H} _ {\mathrm {i}} ^ {*} - \Delta H _ {\mathrm {f}}}{\bar {V} _ {\mathrm {i}}}\right) ^ {1 / 2},
$$

where $\delta_{\mathbf{B}}$ is the square-root of the cohesive density of the solvent, $\Delta H_{f}$ is the enthalpy of fusion, and $V_{i}$ is the molar volume of the solute. Finally, partial molar enthalpies of solution of carbon, titanium, zirconium, thulium, and rhenium were calculated by the equation

$$
\Delta \overline {{\mathrm {H}}} _ {\mathrm {i}} ^ {*} = \mathrm {T} \Delta \overline {{\mathrm {S}}} _ {\mathrm {i}} ^ {*} - \mathrm {R T} \ln \mathrm {N} _ {\mathrm {i}},
$$

where $\Delta \overline{\mathbf{S}}_{\mathbf{i}}^{*}$ is the sum of the entropy terms.

# Introduction

The solubilities of selected elements in liquid plutonium have been of continued interest in this Laboratory. $^{(1,2)}$ The individual solubilities of carbon, titanium, vanadium, chromium, zirconium, niobium, molybdenum, thulium, tantalum, tungsten, and rhenium have been measured under reversible conditions from 700 to $1000^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . The binary systems plutonium-vanadium, -chromium, -niobium, -molybdenum, -tantalum, and -tungsten are simple eutectic-types. $^{(3)}$ The other elements form either compounds or solid solutions with plutonium in the temperature and composition range of interest. $^{(3-5)}$ The solubilities and the solid phases in equilibrium with the liquid solution at $850^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ are given in Table I.

Table I   
Solubilities and Solid Phases of Selected Elements in Liquid Plutonium at $850^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$   

<table><tr><td>Element</td><td>Solid Phase</td><td>Structure Symmetry</td><td>Solubility at 850°C, N × 103</td></tr><tr><td>C</td><td>PuC1-x</td><td>FCC</td><td>26.7</td></tr><tr><td>Ti</td><td>β-Ti in ε-Pu a</td><td></td><td>···</td></tr><tr><td>Ti</td><td>Pu in β-Ti b</td><td></td><td>261</td></tr><tr><td>V</td><td>V</td><td></td><td>22.9</td></tr><tr><td>Cr</td><td>Cr</td><td></td><td>43.5</td></tr><tr><td>Zr</td><td>Zr in Pu</td><td></td><td>80.3</td></tr><tr><td>Nb</td><td>Nb</td><td></td><td>20.5</td></tr><tr><td>Mo</td><td>Mo</td><td></td><td>30.8</td></tr><tr><td>Tm</td><td>PuTm x</td><td>Rhom.</td><td>50.4</td></tr><tr><td>Ta</td><td>Ta</td><td></td><td>1.90</td></tr><tr><td>W</td><td>W</td><td></td><td>0.167</td></tr><tr><td>Re</td><td>PuRe2</td><td>Hex.</td><td>27.5</td></tr></table>

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathbf {\Delta} _ {t <   7 7 0 ^ {\mathrm {o}} \mathbf {C}} ^ {\mathrm {a}} \\ { } ^ { \mathrm { b } } _ { t } > 7 7 0 ^ { \mathrm { o } } \mathrm { C } \\ \end{array}
$$

Partial molar enthalpies of solution can be calculated for these solutes from basic thermodynamic principles provided that several limiting assumptions are adopted. Similar calculations can be applied to other binary systems; for example, partial enthalpies of solution for tungsten and tantalum in the liquid rare-earth metals can be calculated from the solubility measurements on these systems.(6,7) These partial molar enthalpies of solution should prove useful in estimating solubilities and the relative order of solubility in a series of related solvents. The enthalpies should be applicable to corrosion technology, heat transfer studies, casting and processing procedures, and to liquid fuels programs. The estimates are approximate and should not replace measurements where quantitative data are required.

# Discussion

The Derivation of a Solubility Equation. For a binary system composed of solute A and liquid solvent B, the reaction

$$
A (s) + B (\ell) = B (\ell , \text {s a t} ^ {\prime} d \text {w i t h A}) \tag {1}
$$

can be written to represent the equilibrium between excess solid A and liquid solution.

In general, this equilibrium can also be expressed as

$$
\mathrm {R} \ln \mathrm {N} _ {\mathrm {i}} = \left(\Delta \mathrm {S} _ {\mathrm {f}} + \Delta \overline {{\mathrm {S}}} _ {\mathrm {i}} ^ {\mathrm {x s}}\right) - \left(\Delta \mathrm {H} _ {\mathrm {f}} + \Delta \overline {{\mathrm {H}}} _ {\mathrm {i}} ^ {\mathrm {x s}}\right) / \mathrm {T} \quad , \tag {2}
$$

where $R$ is the gas constant, $N_{i}$ is the mole fraction of solute $i$ in a saturated solution at absolute temperature, $T$ , $\Delta S_{f}$ and $\Delta H_{f}$ are the entropy and enthalpy of fusion of $i$ , and $\Delta S_{i}^{XS}$ and $\Delta H_{i}^{XS}$ are the excess entropy and enthalpy of solution of the solute in the solvent of interest.(8)

Equation (2) is valid if the activity coefficient is constant within the limits of composition and temperature under consideration, if the heat capacities of the solid solute and the super-cooled liquid solute are equal at the temperature of interest; and, if the only phase change between the given temperature and that of fusion of the solute is melting.

The enthalpy term in Eq. (2) is a function of composition so that

$$
\Delta \overline {{\mathrm {H}}} _ {\mathrm {i}} ^ {\mathrm {x s}} = (1 - \mathrm {N} _ {\mathrm {i}}) ^ {2} \alpha , \tag {3}
$$

where $\alpha$ is a constant. Consequently, a correction would be necessary if very accurate values of the enthalpy term are desired. For most of these systems, however, this correction can be neglected.

If an empirical approach to solubility theory is adopted, both the excess entropy and enthalpy terms must be evaluated. Equation (2) becomes

$$
\mathrm {R} \ln \mathrm {N} _ {\mathrm {i}} = \mathrm {A} _ {\mathrm {i}} - \mathrm {B} _ {\mathrm {i}} / \mathrm {T}, \tag {4}
$$

where the total entropy of solution $(\Delta S_{\mathbf{f}} + \Delta \overline{S}_{\mathbf{i}}^{\mathrm{XS}})$ is designated as $\mathbf{A_i}$ and the total enthalpy $(\Delta H_{\mathbf{f}} + \Delta H_{\mathbf{i}}^{\mathrm{XS}})$ as $\mathbf{B_i}$ . These constants were calculated from the solubility data for each solute by least squares computations. The standard deviation computed for each equation was less than 0.1 over a three- to ten-fold change in the concentration of the solute in a $250^{\circ}$ to $300^{\circ}$ temperature range. Values for $\mathbf{A_i}$ and $\mathbf{B_i}$ are listed in Table II.

Table II   
Calculated Constants for Empirical Theory in Liquid Plutonium Solutions   

<table><tr><td rowspan="2">Element</td><td colspan="2">Calculated Constants</td></tr><tr><td>Ai</td><td>Bi×10-3</td></tr><tr><td>W</td><td>1.60</td><td>21.2</td></tr><tr><td>Ta</td><td>3.52</td><td>18.0</td></tr><tr><td>Nb</td><td>1.28</td><td>10.0</td></tr><tr><td>V</td><td>3.66</td><td>12.5</td></tr><tr><td>Mo</td><td>1.51</td><td>9.42</td></tr><tr><td>Cr</td><td>6.04</td><td>13.9</td></tr><tr><td>C</td><td>2.39</td><td>10.5</td></tr><tr><td>Tm</td><td>3.37</td><td>10.4</td></tr><tr><td>Re</td><td>0.89</td><td>9.0</td></tr><tr><td>Zr</td><td>13.1</td><td>20.2</td></tr><tr><td>Tia</td><td>8.69</td><td>23.0</td></tr><tr><td>Tib</td><td>3.38</td><td>6.8</td></tr></table>

aIn temperature range 700-750C   
bIn temperature range 800-1000 C

If the regular solution approach is adopted, Eq (2) becomes

$$
R \ln N _ {i} = \Delta S _ {f} - \Delta \bar {H} _ {i} ^ {*} \tag {5}
$$

where $\Delta \overline{\mathrm{H}}_{\mathrm{i}}^{*}$ is the sum of the enthalpy terms.

Quasi-Chemical Approach to Solubility. The excess enthalpy term, $\Delta \overline{H}_{i}^{xs}$ , and the difference between ideal and regular solutions can be illustrated by the quasi-chemical approach to bond energies. $^{(9)}$ It can be shown that

$$
\Delta H ^ {x s} = - z n N _ {i} N _ {B} \left[ E _ {i B} - \left(\frac {E _ {i i} + E _ {B B}}{2}\right) \right], \tag {6}
$$

where $\mathbf{Z}$ is the number of neighbors of each solute atom, $\mathbf{N}$ is Avogadro's number, $\mathbf{N}_{\mathrm{B}}$ is the mole fraction solvent, and $\mathbf{E_{iB}}$ , $\mathbf{E_{ji}}$ , and $\mathbf{E_{BB}}$ represent the potential interaction energies between solute-solvent, solute-solute, and solvent-solvent pairs, respectively. In liquid solutions, $\mathbf{Z}$ is the average value for the coordination number. By differentiation of Eq (6), in dilute solution

$$
\Delta \overline {{\mathrm {H}}} _ {\mathrm {i}} ^ {\mathrm {x s}} = - \mathrm {Z N} \left[ \mathrm {E} _ {\mathrm {i B}} - \left(\frac {\mathrm {E} _ {\mathrm {i i}} + \mathrm {E} _ {\mathrm {B B}}}{2}\right) \right] \tag {7}
$$

In an ideal solution $\mathbf{E_{iB}}$ is equal to the average of $\mathbf{E_{ij}}$ plus $\mathbf{E_{BB}}$ . In regular solutions these terms are not equal and the excess enthalpy of solution will depend upon the relative magnitudes of these interaction energies.

Solubility Parameters. Hildebrand and Scott have proposed solubility parameters based upon relations between molar volumes and bonding energies. (10) Their equation for the partial molar excess enthalpy of solution $\Delta H_{i}^{xs}$ is

$$
\Delta \bar {H} _ {i} ^ {\mathrm {X S}} = \bar {V} _ {i} \left[ \left(\frac {E _ {i} ^ {\mathrm {V}}}{\bar {V} _ {i}}\right) ^ {1 / 2} - \left(\frac {E _ {B} ^ {\mathrm {V}}}{\bar {V} _ {B}}\right) ^ {1 / 2} \right] ^ {2} \rho_ {B} ^ {2} = \bar {V} _ {i} (\delta_ {i} - \delta_ {B}) ^ {2} \rho_ {B} ^ {2} \tag {8}
$$

where $\overline{\mathbf{V}}_{\mathbf{i}}$ and $\overline{\mathbf{V}}_{\mathbf{B}}$ are the molar volumes of solute and solvent; $\mathbf{E}_{\mathbf{i}}^{\mathrm{v}}$ and $\mathbf{E}_{\mathbf{B}}^{\mathrm{v}}$ are the energies of vaporization; and, $\rho_{\mathbf{B}}$ is the volume fraction of $\mathbf{B}$ , which is generally one in dilute solutions. Hildebrand and Scott point out that metal solutions provide an excellent means of testing Eq (8) because of rather large changes in parameters such as the molar volume. This equation, however, will not be valid when interatomic bonding occurs; the basic assumptions in deriving Eq (8) include the validity of the geometric mean law and the dissociation of the solute into the solution. The values of $(\mathbf{E}^{\mathrm{v}} / \overline{\mathbf{V}})^{1/2}$ are commonly designated as solubility parameters; because of the approximations in deriving Eq (8), values of $\Delta \mathbf{H}_{\mathbf{i}}^{\mathrm{xs}}$ calculated by this method will be inexact.

Enthalpies of Solution of Solutes Which Form Simple-Eutectics With Plutonium. The equilibrium reactions of plutonium with vanadium, chromium, niobium, molybdenum, tantalum, and tungsten can be expressed by the equation

$$
A (s) + P u (l) = P u (l, s a t ^ {\prime} d w i t h A), \tag {9}
$$

where $A$ is the solute element i. Measurements of the individual solubilities

of these elements were made upon approach to saturation from both over- and under-saturated conditions. $^{(1,2)}$ The solute concentration at each temperature, over a series of measurements, was independent of the container material and the direction of approach to equilibrium. The variation in average values at each temperature was less than three relative percent.

The entropies of fusion of these solute elements are 2.3 e.u.(11) This value was used with the solubility data to calculate the partial enthalpies of solution by Eq (5). The calculated enthalpies were independent of temperature. Average values are given in Table III. The average correction factors in the enthalpies, as calculated by Eq (3), are also given in this Table. This correction is insignificant for tungsten and tantalum; it is 0.2 kcal/mole for niobium and molybdenum; 0.3 kcal/mole for vanadium; and 0.4 kcal/mole for chromium. Thus, the correction is less than 5 percent in every case.

Table III   
The Partial Molar Enthalpies and Solubility Parameters of Tungsten, Tantalum, Niobium, Vanadium, Molybdenum, and Chromium in Liquid Plutonium   

<table><tr><td>Element</td><td>ΔH*, kcal/mole</td><td>(1-Ni)2, avg</td><td>ΔHf, kcal/mole(a)</td><td>δi, calc</td><td>k(b)</td></tr><tr><td>W</td><td>22.0 ± 0.1</td><td>1.00</td><td>8.4</td><td>119</td><td>0.8</td></tr><tr><td>Ta</td><td>16.6 ± 0.1</td><td>1.00</td><td>7.5</td><td>110</td><td>0.8</td></tr><tr><td>Nb</td><td>11.1 ± 0.1</td><td>0.96</td><td>6.3</td><td>102</td><td>0.8</td></tr><tr><td>V</td><td>11.0 ± 0.1</td><td>0.95</td><td>5.1</td><td>107</td><td>0.9</td></tr><tr><td>Mo</td><td>10.3 ± 0.1</td><td>0.94</td><td>6.7</td><td>101</td><td>0.8</td></tr><tr><td>Cr</td><td>9.5 ± 0.1</td><td>0.90</td><td>5.0</td><td>106</td><td>0.9</td></tr></table>

(a) From Ref. (11)   
(b) $\mathrm{k} = \delta_{\mathrm{i}} \mathrm{calc} / (\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{V}} / \overline{\mathrm{V}}_{\mathrm{i}})^{1/2}$

Values of the solubility parameters of these solutes, $\delta_{\mathrm{i}}$ calc, were calculated from the enthalpies of solution and the enthalpies of fusion(11) by the expression

$$
\delta_ {i c a l c} = \delta_ {P u} ^ {O} + \left[ \left(\Delta \bar {H} _ {i} ^ {*} - \Delta H _ {f}\right) / \bar {V} _ {i} \right] ^ {1 / 2}. \tag {10}
$$

where $\delta_{\mathbf{pu}}^{\mathrm{o}}$ is 81.(12) These values are less than $(\mathrm{E}^{\mathrm{V}} / \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{i}})^{1 / 2}$ . A relative constant $k_{i}$ was calculated for each element to correlate the $\delta_{i}$ calc to $(\mathrm{E}^{\mathrm{V}} / \mathrm{V})^{1 / 2}$ by the equation

$$
k = \delta_ {i c a l c} / \left(E _ {i} ^ {V} / \bar {V} _ {i}\right) ^ {1 / 2}. \tag {11}
$$

The values of $\delta_{\mathrm{icalc}}$ and $\mathbf{k}_{\mathrm{i}}$ also are shown in Table III.

Enthalpies of Solution of Tantalum and Tungsten in Liquid Rare-Earth Solvents. The equilibrium reactions between tungsten or tantalum and the liquid rare-earth metals probably correspond to

$$
\mathrm {W} (\mathrm {s}) + \mathrm {S} (\mathrm {l}) = \mathrm {S} (\mathrm {l}, \text {s a t} ^ {\prime} \mathrm {d} \text {w i t h W}), \text {a n d} \tag {12}
$$

$$
\mathrm {T a} (\mathrm {s}) + \mathrm {S} (\ell) = \mathrm {S} (\ell , \text {s a t} ^ {\prime} \mathrm {d} \text {w i t h T a}), \tag {13}
$$

where S is the rare-earth metal solvent. Dennison, Tschetter, and Gschneidner measured the solubilities of tungsten and tantalum in sixteen rare-earth metals over the temperature range 1325 to $2150^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . Using their data, enthalpies of solution were calculated by Eq (5). Average enthalpies are given in Table IV. The solubility increases in order from ytterbium to scandium as shown by decreasing $\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{i}}^{*}$ values for both solutes except for one-position changes for tantalum in terbium and holmium. Tungsten is less soluble than tantalum in each solvent.

Values of the solubility parameters of these solvents, $\delta_{\mathrm{s}}$ calc, were calculated from the enthalpies of solution by the equation

$$
\delta_ {\mathrm {s c a l c}} = \delta_ {\mathrm {i c a l c}} + \left[ \left(\Delta \overline {{\mathrm {H}}} _ {\mathrm {i}} ^ {*} - \Delta \mathrm {H} _ {\mathrm {f}}\right) / \overline {{\mathrm {V}}} _ {\mathrm {i}} \right] ^ {1 / 2} \tag {14}
$$

The values of $\delta_{\mathrm{scalc}}$ are compared with $(\mathrm{E}^{\mathrm{V}\mathrm{s}} / \overline{\mathrm{V}}_{\mathrm{s}})^{1 / 2}$ values in Table V.

Enthalpies of Elements Which Form Compounds with Plutonium. Plutonium reacts with carbon, thulium, and rhenium to form compounds in the solid phase over the temperature and composition range of interest.(3) If the reference state in these systems is the supercooled liquid solute and the solute is treated as dissociated in the liquid solution, the reaction may be written as:

$$
\mathrm {X A} (\ell , \text {s u p e r c o o l e d}) + \mathrm {Y P u} (\ell) = \mathrm {P u} _ {\mathrm {y}} \mathrm {A} _ {\mathrm {x}} (s), \tag {15}
$$

and

$$
\mathrm {P u} _ {\mathrm {y}} \mathrm {A} _ {\mathrm {x}} (\mathrm {s}) + \mathrm {P u} (\ell) = \mathrm {P u} (\ell , \text {s a t} ^ {\prime} \mathrm {d} \text {w i t h A}). \tag {16}
$$

Equation (5) is valid if the entropy changes that occur in going from supercooled liquid to compound to the hypothetical solid A in equilibrium

with solution are negligible. The enthalpies of solution calculated under this assumption were independent of temperature; the average deviation in the value as a function of temperature was less than 0.1 kcal/mole. The average enthalpies are listed in Table VI. Average correction factors for these enthalpies, as calculated by Eq (3), are also given in this Table. The geometric mean law is not valid so that no attempt has been made to calculate solubility parameters for these systems.

Table VI   
The Partial Molar Enthalpies of Solution for Carbon, Thulium, and Rhenium in Liquid Plutonium   

<table><tr><td>Element</td><td>ΔH*, kcal/mole</td><td>(1 - N2)2, avg.</td></tr><tr><td>C</td><td>10.5 ± 0.1</td><td>0.94</td></tr><tr><td>Tm</td><td>9.2 ± 0.2</td><td>0.90</td></tr><tr><td>Re</td><td>10.5 ± 0.1</td><td>0.95</td></tr></table>

Enthalpies of Elements Which Form Solid Solutions with Plutonium. Both zirconium and titanium form solid solutions with plutonium from 700 to $1000^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . The equilibrium reaction for zirconium is

$$
\epsilon - \mathrm {P u} (\mathrm {s}, \text {s a t} ^ {\prime} \mathrm {d} \text {w i t h Z r}) + \mathrm {P u} (\ell) = \mathrm {P u} (\ell , \text {s a t} ^ {\prime} \mathrm {d} \text {w i t h Z r}); \tag {17}
$$

for titanium, the reaction below $770^{\circ}C$ is

$$
\epsilon - \mathrm {P u} (\mathrm {s}, \text {s a t} ^ {\prime} \mathrm {d} \text {w i t h} \mathrm {T i}) + \mathrm {P u} (\lambda) = \mathrm {P u} (\lambda , \text {s a t} ^ {\prime} \mathrm {d} \text {w i t h} \mathrm {T i}); \tag {18}
$$

and for titanium above $770^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$

$$
\beta - \mathrm {T i} (\mathrm {s}, \text {s a t} ^ {\prime} \mathrm {d} \text {w i t h P u}) + \mathrm {P u} (\lambda) = \mathrm {P u} (\lambda , \text {s a t} ^ {\prime} \mathrm {d} \text {w i t h T i}). \tag {19}
$$

Since the excess enthalpy terms are absorbed in $\Delta \overline{\mathbf{H}}_{\mathbf{i}}^{*}$ , the principle thermodynamic difference in these solutions from simple eutectic systems can be attributed to the configurational entropy in the solid solution. This term is - R ln $\mathbf{N}_{\mathbf{i}}^{\mathbf{s}}$ , where $\mathbf{N}_{\mathbf{i}}^{\mathbf{s}}$ is the mole fraction of solute i in a saturated solid solution at a given temperature. The derivation of the basic solubility equation under these conditions results in the expression

$$
\Delta \bar {H} _ {i} ^ {* *} = T \Delta S _ {f} - R \left[ \ln N _ {i} ^ {S} - \ln N _ {i} \right] \quad , \tag {20}
$$

where $\Delta \overline{\mathrm{H}}_{\mathrm{i}}^{**}$ is equal to $\Delta \overline{\mathrm{H}}_{\mathrm{i}}^{\mathrm{XS}} / [1 - \mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{i}}]^2 + \Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{f}}$

Values for $\mathbf{N}_{\mathrm{i}}^{\mathrm{s}}$ for zirconium and titanium were estimated from the phase diagrams. (4,5,13) Partial molar enthalpies of liquid solution $\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{i}}^{**}$ were calculated by Eq (20). The enthalpy of the plutonium-zirconium system was $4.2 \pm 0.1 \mathrm{kcal} / \mathrm{mole}$ from 700 to $950^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ . The enthalpy of liquid solution for the titanium-plutonium system was $4.1 \pm 0.1 \mathrm{kcal} / \mathrm{mole}$ from 700 to $750^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $5.3 \pm 0.1 \mathrm{kcal} / \mathrm{mole}$ from 800 to $1000^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ . The average correction factors for these enthalpies, as calculated by Eq (3), range from 0.5 to 0.8. However, estimates of solid phase solubilities are also approximate, and the $\Delta \mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{i}}^{*}$ values are limited to the significance of Eq (20). The shift in the titanium enthalpy was attributed to the change in the solid phase at $770^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ . Solubility parameters were not calculated because of the formation of solid solutions in these systems.

# Conclusions

In this paper, enthalpy values have been calculated from solubility data by utilizing regular solution theory. Since solubility is an exponential function of the difference between the entropy and enthalpy divided by the absolute temperature, small errors in the solubility data may result in significant deviations in the latter. The approximate enthalpies can be used for qualitative estimates of solubility; refined estimates are possible with the correction factors. However, it remains necessary to measure the solubilities under the conditions of interest to obtain quantitative data.

The author thanks J.A. Leary for his advice and encouragement during this study.

# BASIC DATA AND

# THERMODYNAMIC PROPERTIES, II

Chairman: Irving Johnson

Argonne National Laboratory

Argonne, Illinois, U.S.A.

THEORETICAL CONCEPTS FOR HIGH TEMPERATURE TERNARY MOLTEN

SYSTEMS AND THEIR IMPLICATIONS IN PYROMETALLURGICAL PROCESSES

Milton Blander and Kjell Hagemark  
Science Center, North American Rockwell Corporation  
Thousand Oaks, California 91360  
U.S.A.

# Abstract

Fundamental theories of ternary systems will be reviewed with a discussion of the similarities and differences between ternary reciprocal systems $(A^{+},B^{+} / X^{-},Y^{-})$ additive ternary systems $(A^{+},B^{+},C^{+} / X^{-})$ and metallic systems. In dilute solutions of two components one may define association constants between pairs of ions in terms of fundamental energy parameters. In concentrated solutions, deviations from ideal solution behavior are related to non-random mixing. Theoretical criteria for the prediction of phase diagram behavior, solubilities, extraction behavior, liquid-liquid miscibility, and other thermodynamic properties important in pyrometallurgical processing will be presented for simple systems.

# Introduction

Pyrometallurgical processing is generally carried out in multicomponent systems and an understanding of theoretical concepts is necessary to define and optimize processing methods. In this review we shall first discuss theories of ternary systems, which are the simplest members of the class of multicomponent systems. Our discussion applies equally to molten salt systems and to metallic systems for which there have been parallel developments of fundamental concepts. The extension of the concepts discussed to higher order multicomponent systems is straightforward. In the last section we will present some examples of chemical behavior in systems which illustrate the theoretical concepts.

Ternary molten salt systems consist of four ions: a system consisting of two cations and two anions $(A^{+}, B^{+} / X^{-}, Y^{-})$ is a ternary reciprocal system and one consisting of three cations and one anion $(A^{+}, B^{+}, C^{+} / X^{-})$ or three anions and one cation $(A^{+} / X^{-}, Y^{-}Z^{-})$ is an additive ternary system. The metallic systems which are analogous to these can be understood if one formally considers electrons (e-) as anions. Then interstitial alloys $(A^{+}, B^{+} / e^{-}, X^{-})$ are equivalent to reciprocal systems and substitutional alloys $(A^{+}, B^{+}, C^{+} / e^{-})$ are equivalent to additive systems. The theories which have been developed for molten salts and for alloy systems should exhibit many similarities and aside from specific differences in the models, many of the derived relationships are equivalent. We shall discuss thermodynamic theories for phenomena as phase diagram behavior, solubilities, extraction, and liquid-liquid miscibility which are important in pyrometallurgical processing.

# Definitions

To establish a framework for discussion several quantities need to be defined. The chemical behavior of a component in solution is, of course, determined by its chemical potential, $\mu$ , which has the undesirable mathematical property that it approaches $-\infty$ as the concentration of the component approaches zero. This property is circumvented by defining another quantity, the activity, $a$ , which usually lies in the range of 0 to 1

$$
\mu = \mu^ {o} + R T \ln a
$$

where $\mu^{\circ}$ is the chemical potential of the pure component. To make the definition of activity more meaningful one must introduce relationships which cannot be derived purely thermodynamically but require the introduction of statistical concepts. The simplest relationship for molten salts depends on the definition of an ideal solution as first discussed by Temkin.(1) The activity of an ideal solution of a salt $A_{m}^{X}$ is defined as

$$
a _ {i d} = N _ {A} ^ {m} N _ {X} ^ {n}
$$

where $\mathbb{N}_{\mathbf{A}}$ is the cation fraction of A and $\mathbb{N}_{\mathbf{X}}$ is the anion fraction of X

$$
N _ {A} = \frac {n _ {A}}{\Sigma n} \quad N _ {X} = \frac {n _ {X}}{\Sigma n}
$$

where $n$ are the number of moles of the ions indicated. For interstitial alloys there are analogous relations. For additive ternary systems and substitutional alloys either $N_A$ or $N_X$ is unity.

All real solutions deviate from ideal solutions. This deviation is characterized by the activity coefficient $\gamma$ such that

$$
\gamma = \left(a / a _ {i d}\right) = \left(a / N _ {A} ^ {m} N _ {X} ^ {n}\right)
$$

If the ideal solution is defined in a way which is consistent with the limiting laws, then $\gamma$ will always be a positive finite number. In addition to the definition of $\gamma$ there are several other quantities which are often utilized to characterize deviations from ideal solution behavior:

$$
\text {T h e m o l t e r e x c e s s f r e e e n e r g y} \quad G ^ {\mathrm {E}} = \begin{array}{c} \mathrm {R T} \Sigma \mathrm {N} _ {i} \not \ln \gamma_ {i} \\ \text {a l l} \\ \text {c o m p o n e n t s} \end{array}
$$

The partial molar excess free energy of a component i

$$
\bar {G} _ {i} ^ {E} = R T \ln \gamma_ {i}
$$

The molar enthalpy of mixing $\Delta H_{m} = H - \Sigma N_{i}H_{i}$ and the partial molar enthalpy of solution

$$
\bar {H} _ {i} ^ {E} = \frac {\partial (\bar {G} _ {i} ^ {E} / T)}{\partial (1 / T)} = \left(\frac {\partial \Delta H _ {m}}{\partial n _ {i}}\right)
$$

All these quantities are interrelated and often parallel each other.

# Solutions Dilute in Two Components

The thermodynamic solubility products $(\mathsf{K}_{\mathsf{SP}})$ and enthalpies of solution $(\Delta H_{\mathrm{soln.}})$ of an insoluble salt can be predicted for reciprocal systems and interstitial alloy systems by the use of simple cycle first proposed by Flood, Førland, and Grjothiem. As an

example, the process of dissolution of a solute such as silver chloride in an alkali nitrate

$\mathsf{AgCl}$ (solid or liquid) $\Rightarrow \mathsf{AgCl}$ ( $\infty$ dilution in $\mathsf{KNO}_3$ )

can be broken up into three steps

$\mathrm{AgCl}$ (solid or liquid) $+\mathrm{MNO}_3$ (liquid) $\rightarrow$ MC(solid or liquid)

$$
+ \mathrm {A g N O} _ {3} (\text {l i q u i d}) \tag {1}
$$

$$
\mathrm {M C l} (\text {s o l i d o r l i q u i d}) \rightarrow \mathrm {M C l} (\infty \text {d i l u t i o n i n} \mathrm {M N O} _ {3}) \tag {2}
$$

$$
\mathrm {A g N O} _ {3} (\text {l i q u i d}) \rightarrow \mathrm {A g N O} _ {3} (\infty \text {d i l u t i o n i n M N O} _ {3}) \tag {3}
$$

Consequently

$$
- \mathrm {R T} \ln K _ {\mathrm {S P}} = \Delta G _ {1} + \Delta G _ {2} ^ {\mathrm {S T}} + \Delta G _ {3} ^ {\mathrm {S T}} \tag {4}
$$

and

$$
\Delta \mathrm {H} _ {\text {s o l n .}} = \Delta \mathrm {H} _ {1} + \Delta \mathrm {H} _ {2} + \Delta \mathrm {H} _ {3} \tag {5}
$$

where the superscript ST signifies standard states. If data are available for the reaction (1), then one may calculate $K_{\mathrm{SP}}$ and $\Delta H_{\mathrm{soln}}$ from a knowledge of steps (2) and (3) which involve only binary systems. The complexity of the problem is thus reduced from that of understanding a large number of ternary systems to understanding a much smaller number of binary systems for which there are generally more available data or where reasonable estimates may often be made. The use of this cycle for calculating $K_{\mathrm{SP}}$ and $\Delta H_{\mathrm{soln}}$ has been tested for the dissolution of silver halides in molten alkali nitrates. The results in Table I show good agreement between measured and calculated values.

A similar cycle can be applied to metallic systems, e.g. to the calculation of the solubility product of the oxides of one metal in solution in another metal. This cycle is most useful where step (1) makes the largest contributions to the totals in equations (4) and (5).

Only for special cases in which activity coefficient corrections are negligible can one deduce solubilities directly from solubility products. Generally, the ions of insoluble salts tend to associate in solution and form clusters and ion pairs. This leads to significant negative deviations from ideal solution behavior which must be taken into account in order to calculate truesolubilities. Figure 1 illustrates this for a case in which the corrections are relatively small. If a solution of $\mathsf{Ag}^+$ and $\mathsf{Cl}^-$ ion in molten $\mathsf{KNO}_3$ were ideal, then the solubility of $\mathsf{AgCl}$ would be equal to the activity of $\mathsf{AgNO}_3$ and of $\mathsf{KCl}$ (the activities

![](images/6287d2c1161fae31a4f511f3c6a6d58902c85eae657901de8b4ead7cbf343ee3.jpg)  
1. Solubilities and Activities in Molten $\mathsf{KNO}_3$ (Ref. 3)

![](images/65170efe64ab87c13e89b81a6d4eb4b2daf748d72228799efdb0b70e7924ed69.jpg)  
2. Model for the Definition of $\Delta A_{1}$ , the Specific Bond Free Energy for the Ion Pair $A^{+} - X^{-}$ .

Table I.   
Measured Values of -RT ln Ksp, $\Delta H_{\mathrm{soln}}$ , and Their Comparison with Calculated Values(3)(4)   

<table><tr><td rowspan="2">Solute</td><td rowspan="2">Solvent</td><td colspan="2">-RT ln Ksp, kcal./mole</td><td colspan="2">ΔH, kcal./mole</td></tr><tr><td>Measured</td><td>Calcd.</td><td>Measured</td><td>Calcd.</td></tr><tr><td>AgI</td><td>NaNO3</td><td>29.5</td><td>31.5</td><td>29.5</td><td>29.8</td></tr><tr><td>AgI</td><td>KNO3</td><td>26.7</td><td>28.2</td><td></td><td>27.0</td></tr><tr><td>AgBr</td><td>NaNO3-KNO3</td><td>21.7</td><td>22.1</td><td>22.4</td><td>22.9</td></tr><tr><td>AgI</td><td>NaNO3-KNO3</td><td>28.4</td><td>29.9</td><td>27.9</td><td>28.4</td></tr><tr><td>AgCl2</td><td>KNO3</td><td>17.0</td><td>17.8</td><td>19.2</td><td>19.6</td></tr></table>

are based on standard states such that $\gamma = 1$ at infinite dilution). The solubility of AgCl is higher than these activities as the result of the formation of associated species in solution as

$$
\mathrm {A g} ^ {+} + \mathrm {C l} ^ {-} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm {A g C l} \tag {6}
$$

$$
\mathrm {A g C l} + \mathrm {C l} ^ {-} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm {A g C l} _ {2} ^ {-} \tag {7}
$$

$$
\mathrm {A g C l} + \mathrm {A g} ^ {+} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm {A g} _ {2} \mathrm {C l} ^ {+} \tag {8}
$$

which make the activity coefficients of $\mathrm{AgNO_3}$ and KC2 in solution less than unity. The fact that the plotted activities of $\mathrm{AgNO_3}$ are higher than those of KC2, even though the concentration of Ag equals that of Cl $^-$ , indicates that equilibria as (7) which form species containing more chloride ions than silver ions have larger association constants than equilibria as (8). In many other cases the activity coefficients are so low that solubilities are orders of magnitude higher than for an ideal solution. This is true not only for reciprocal molten salt systems but also for ternary interstitial metallic systems. Consequently, it is important to understand association equilibria in dilute solutions and the influence of these equilibria on the concentration dependence of activity coefficients.

Let us consider a solvent BY dilute in $\mathbf{A}^{+}$ and $\mathbf{X}^{-}$ ions. For metallic systems, $\mathbf{Y}^{-}$ is an electron. The association of $\mathbf{A}^{+}$ and $\mathbf{X}^{-}$ to form an $\mathbf{A}^{+}-\mathbf{X}^{-}$ pair may be visualized as in the two dimensional representation of Figure 2. The interchange of the circled $\mathbf{X}^{-}$ and $\mathbf{Y}^{-}$ ions gives rise to a specific bond free energy change $\Delta A_{1}$ . From the statistical mechanical analysis of a quasi-lattice model it can be shown that $\mathbf{K}_{1}$ , the association constant for the equilibrium $\mathbf{A}^{+}+\mathbf{X}^{-} \rightleftharpoons \mathbf{AX}$ in Figure 2 is given by (5)(6)(7)

$$
\mathrm {K} _ {1} = \mathrm {Z} \left(\exp \left(- \Delta \mathrm {A} _ {1} / \mathrm {R T}\right) - 1\right) \tag {9}
$$

where $\mathbb{Z}$ is a coordination number which ranges from about 4-6 for molten salts. In reciprocal systems and interstitial systems, $\Delta A_{1}$ , is largely related to the exchange of nearest neighbors. In simple systems where long range interactions do not predominate and where $A^{+}$ and $X^{-}$ are simple spherical ions one might expect that $\Delta A_{1}$ is independent of temperature and is truly an energy $\Delta E_{1}$ . In order to test this, one must make measurements over a wide range of temperatures.

The calculation of $\mathbf{K}_1$ from experimental data on the activity coefficients of the components AY or BX can be simply made for the case in which all components and species at low concentrations obey Henry's law. Then it can be shown that

$$
\ln \gamma_ {A Y} = - K _ {1} N _ {X} + \left(K _ {1} K _ {2} - \frac {1}{2} K _ {1} ^ {2}\right) N _ {X} ^ {2} + \left(2 K _ {1} K _ {1 2} - K _ {1} ^ {2}\right) N _ {A} N _ {X} + \dots . \tag {10}
$$

and

$$
\ln \gamma_ {B X} = - K _ {1} N _ {A} + \left(K _ {1} K _ {1 2} - \frac {1}{2} K _ {1} ^ {2}\right) N _ {A} ^ {2} + \left(2 K _ {1} K _ {2} - K _ {1} ^ {2}\right) N _ {A} N _ {X} + \dots . \tag {11}
$$

and by comparison with a MacLaurin series expansion one can derive a method for evaluating $\mathrm{K}_1(5)$ from limiting slopes

$$
- K _ {1} = \lim  _ {\substack {N _ {A} \rightarrow 0\\N _ {X} \rightarrow 0}} \left(\frac {\partial \ln \gamma_ {B X}}{\partial N _ {A}}\right) = \lim  _ {\substack {N _ {A} \rightarrow 0\\N _ {X} \rightarrow 0}} \left(\frac {\partial \ln \gamma_ {A Y}}{\partial N _ {X}}\right) \tag{12}
$$

For metallic solutions $\mathbf{K}_1$ is the negative of the atomic interaction coefficients introduced by Wagner(9) and Chipman and Eliott(10). Figure 3 exhibits some of the data for the system $\mathrm{Ag}^{+} + \mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ in $\mathrm{NaNO}_3$ which have been utilized in conjunction with equation (12) to evaluate $\mathbf{K}_1$ for the formation of $\mathrm{Ag}^{+} - \mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ ion pairs over a range of temperatures.

Values of $\Delta A_{1}$ derived from such measurements at several temperatures (11)(12)(13) are given in Table II and are independent of temperature within experimental errors for all reasonable values of the coordination number. Consequently, equation (9) can be used to predict the temperature coefficients of $K_{1}$ where $A^{+}$ and $X^{-}$ are simple spherical ions. Similar data have been obtained for metallic systems as shown in Figures 4 and $5^{(6)}$ and Table III(14) where the interaction coefficients $k_{i,j} = -K_{1}$ . An extensive check of the temperature dependence of $\Delta A_{1}$ for metallic systems has not been made but $\Delta A_{1}$ appears to be temperature independent in some of the measurements which have been made (see Figures 4 and 5 from reference 6). More complete tests would help to confirm this. The quasi lattice model also leads to expressions for the higher association constants (5)(7) which are somewhat more complex.

Similar equations hold for additive molten salt systems and for substitutional alloys (7)(15) with a modification in the definition of $\Delta A_{1}$ which is the specific free energy change for the exchange of next nearest neighbors (considering electrons as anions). This modification is illustrated in the two dimensional model in Figure 6 which illustrates the association of next nearest neighbor cations

$$
\mathrm {B} ^ {+} + \mathrm {C} ^ {+} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm {B} ^ {+} - \mathrm {C} ^ {+}
$$

An important conclusion may be deduced from equation (9). If $\Delta A_{1}$ is zero, then $K_{1}$ is zero. If $\Delta A_{1}$ is negative, then the two ions involved associate and $K_{1}$ can be a large number if $\Delta A_{1}$ is very negative. On the other hand, if $\Delta A_{1}$ is positive, it means that the "associating" ions repel each other which leads to negative values of $K_{1}$ . This peculiarity is paralleled by the behavior of second virial coefficients of some gases (which are the negative of association constants) and

$$
\begin{array}{c c c c c c} \mathrm {A} ^ {+} & \mathrm {X} ^ {-} & \mathrm {A} ^ {+} & & \mathrm {A} ^ {+} & \mathrm {X} ^ {-} & \mathrm {A} ^ {+} \end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{array}{c c c c c c c} \mathrm {x} ^ {-} & & \mathrm {B} ^ {+} & & \mathrm {x} ^ {-} & & + \\ & & & & & \mathrm {x} ^ {-} & & \mathrm {c} ^ {+} \\ & & & & & \mathrm {x} ^ {-} \end{array}
$$

$$
\mathrm {A} ^ {+} \quad \mathrm {X} ^ {-} \quad \mathrm {A} ^ {+} \quad \mathrm {A} ^ {+} \quad \mathrm {X} ^ {-} \quad \mathrm {A} ^ {+}
$$

$$
\updownarrow \downarrow
$$

$$
\begin{array}{c c c c c c} \mathrm {A} ^ {+} & \mathrm {X} ^ {-} & \mathrm {C} ^ {+} & & \mathrm {A} ^ {+} & \mathrm {X} ^ {-} & \mathrm {A} ^ {+} \end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{array}{c c c c c c c} \mathrm {X} ^ {-} & \mathrm {B} ^ {+} & \mathrm {X} ^ {-} & + & \mathrm {X} ^ {-} & \mathrm {A} ^ {+} & \mathrm {X} ^ {-} \end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{array}{c c c c c c c} \mathrm {A} ^ {+} & \mathrm {X} ^ {-} & \mathrm {A} ^ {+} & \mathrm {A} ^ {+} & \mathrm {X} ^ {-} & \mathrm {A} ^ {+} \end{array}
$$

6. Two Dimensional Model for the Association of Next Nearest Neighbor.

Table II.   
Values of $\Delta A_{1}$ Obtained From the Comparison of Theory with Experimental Data(11)(12)(13)
